title: Noise and Randomness
primer: GPUs have no built-in random number generator, so shader programmers use deterministic hash functions that look random but are perfectly reproducible. From a simple hash you can build value noise (smooth random values on a grid), and from multiple octaves of noise you get fractal Brownian motion (fBm) — the mathematical pattern behind clouds, terrain, fire, and most natural textures. This lesson builds all three layers from scratch.

challenges.title: Hash function (TV static)
challenges.description: Implement a 2D hash function that maps any integer grid coordinate to a pseudo-random value in [0, 1]. Apply it to every pixel's tile coordinate to produce TV-static noise that changes every frame.
hints: Complete the hash by returning `fract(p.x * p.y)` — multiplying two well-scrambled floats and taking the fractional part produces values that pass a visual randomness test.
Pass `p` (the scaled + time-offset UV) into `hash(p)`. The large scale (200.0) ensures adjacent pixels have very different integer coordinates, giving uncorrelated noise values.
challenges.unlocks_theory: This hash works by exploiting floating-point arithmetic: multiplying by large primes scrambles the bits of the input, and `fract` discards the integer part to produce a value in [0,1]. The result is deterministic (same input always gives the same output) but aperiodic within the visible range — the two properties that define a good shader hash. Quality can be improved with extra mixing rounds at the cost of GPU instructions.

challenges.title: Value noise (smooth noise)
challenges.description: Build smooth value noise by sampling a hash at the four corners of each grid cell and interpolating with smoothstep. The result is continuous smooth blobs instead of per-pixel static.
hints: `vec2 u = smoothstep(0.0, 1.0, f)` applies a cubic Hermite curve to the local fractional position, giving C1-continuous interpolation. Plain `mix` with a linear `f` would produce visible gradient discontinuities at cell borders.
The bilinear interpolation `mix(mix(a, b, u.x), mix(c, d, u.x), u.y)` first interpolates along x on both rows, then interpolates those two results along y. Pass `p` into `valueNoise(p)` in `main`.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Value noise interpolates random values defined on a grid. The key to smoothness is using a cubic (C1-continuous) interpolant — `smoothstep` or the quintic `6t⁵-15t⁴+10t³` — instead of plain linear `mix`. The cubic interpolant guarantees that the derivative is zero at grid points, eliminating the faceted look of linear interpolation. This is essentially the algorithm Ken Perlin used in his original 1985 paper (though Perlin noise uses gradient vectors rather than scalar values for even smoother results).

challenges.title: Fractal Brownian Motion (fBm)
challenges.description: Stack multiple octaves of value noise at increasing frequencies and decreasing amplitudes to produce fBm — the mathematical pattern behind clouds and terrain. Each octave doubles the frequency and halves the amplitude.
hints: Each octave adds `amplitude * valueNoise(p * frequency)` to the accumulated value. After each octave, halve the amplitude (`*= 0.5`) and double the frequency (`*= 2.0`).

With amplitude starting at 0.5 and halving each octave, the maximum possible sum across 5 octaves is 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.0625 + 0.03125 = ~0.97, so the output stays naturally in [0,1] — no clamping needed.
challenges.unlocks_theory: fBm (fractal Brownian motion) is a self-similar signal: it looks the same at any scale because each zoom level contributes the same relative amount of detail. The ratio of amplitude decay to frequency doubling is called the *Hurst exponent* — 0.5 (halving amplitude per octave) gives a natural H=0.5 which matches many real-world surfaces. Increasing the amplitude multiplier above 0.5 makes the surface rougher; decreasing it makes it smoother.

challenges.title: Domain-warped cloud texture
challenges.description: Apply domain warping to fBm: compute a first pass of noise and use it to distort the input coordinates of a second pass. This creates the swirling, organic shapes found in clouds and lava. Combine with the animated cosine palette for a vivid result.
hints: Domain warping: use `warp` (a vec2 of two independent noise values) to offset `p` before the final fBm call: `float n = fbm(p + warp * 1.5);`. The offset magnitude (1.5) controls how twisted the result looks.

For the cosine palette: `0.5 + 0.5 * cos(6.28318 * vec3(n, n + 0.33, n + 0.67))` cycles through hues by phase-shifting the three color channels. Larger phase differences make more colorful, less monochromatic output.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Domain warping (also called 'feedback noise' or 'warp noise') was popularized by Inigo Quilez. Instead of evaluating noise at the raw coordinates, you first compute a noise-derived offset and add it to the coordinates. The result has swirling, turbulent structures that look nothing like the underlying grid. Multiple warp passes create increasingly complex organic patterns — used in production for everything from VFX fire to procedural terrain texturing.
